sections.heading: Complications
sections.content: Disease-related: Bowel obstruction or perforation, Anemia and nutritional deficiency, Local invasion causing fistulae or organ dysfunction. Treatment-related: Chemotherapy: Hand-foot syndrome (capecitabine), Peripheral sensory neuropathy (oxaliplatin) - may be chronic and cumulative; discontinuation should be considered after 3-4 months or for unacceptable neurotoxicity. Duloxetine may be considered for painful neuropathy., Myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia), Diarrhea, mucositis (fluoropyrimidines, irinotecan), Hepatotoxicity (oxaliplatin, irinotecan), Nausea/vomiting. Immunotherapy: Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) - management per NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities., Colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, dermatologic reactions. Targeted therapy: Hypertension, proteinuria, impaired wound healing (bevacizumab) - should be held for at least 28 days prior to elective surgery., Acneiform rash, hypomagnesemia (EGFR inhibitors: cetuximab, panitumumab), Diarrhea (anti-angiogenic agents: ziv-aflibercept, ramucirumab), Increased risk of pulmonary hemorrhage, especially for ultracentral tumors, with combination of bevacizumab and SBRT. Surgery: Anastomotic leak, Wound infection, Urinary retention/injury, Sexual dysfunction (erectile dysfunction, ejaculatory dysfunction), Bowel dysfunction (anterior resection syndrome - urgency, frequency, clustering), Stoma-related complications (if permanent colostomy after APR). Radiation: Acute: radiation dermatitis, cystitis, proctitis, diarrhea, fatigue, Late: bowel obstruction, fistula, sexual dysfunction, pelvic fractures/decreased bone density, vaginal stenosis, Small bowel toxicity (dose constraints: Dmax 55 Gy, V45 Gy ≤150 cc for individual loops).

labels: Bowel obstruction or perforation
Anemia and nutritional deficiency
Local invasion causing fistulae or organ dysfunction
Hand-foot syndrome (capecitabine)

Peripheral sensory neuropathy (oxaliplatin) - may be chronic and cumulative; discontinuation should be considered after 3-4 months or for unacceptable neurotoxicity. Duloxetine may be considered for painful neuropathy.
Myelosuppression (neutropenia, thrombocytopenia)
Diarrhea, mucositis (fluoropyrimidines, irinotecan)
Hepatotoxicity (oxaliplatin, irinotecan)
Nausea/vomiting
Immune-related adverse events (irAEs) - management per NCCN Guidelines for Management of Immune Checkpoint Inhibitor-Related Toxicities.
Colitis, pneumonitis, hepatitis, endocrinopathies, dermatologic reactions.
Hypertension, proteinuria, impaired wound healing (bevacizumab) - should be held for at least 28 days prior to elective surgery.
Acneiform rash, hypomagnesemia (EGFR inhibitors: cetuximab, panitumumab)
Diarrhea (anti-angiogenic agents: ziv-aflibercept, ramucirumab)
Increased risk of pulmonary hemorrhage, especially for ultracentral tumors, with combination of bevacizumab and SBRT.
Anastomotic leak
Wound infection
Urinary retention/injury
Sexual dysfunction (erectile dysfunction, ejaculatory dysfunction)
Bowel dysfunction (anterior resection syndrome - urgency, frequency, clustering)
Stoma-related complications (if permanent colostomy after APR)
Acute: radiation dermatitis, cystitis, proctitis, diarrhea, fatigue
Late: bowel obstruction, fistula, sexual dysfunction, pelvic fractures/decreased bone density, vaginal stenosis
Small bowel toxicity (dose constraints: Dmax 55 Gy, V45 Gy ≤150 cc for individual loops)

cards.name: Bowel obstruction or perforation
cards.detail: May require emergent surgical intervention, diverting ostomy, or stenting for palliation. Bevacizumab use in the setting of colon/rectal stents may increase the risk of perforation.

cards.name: Anemia and nutritional deficiency
cards.detail: Supportive care including iron supplementation, transfusion as needed, and nutritional support.

cards.name: Local invasion causing fistulae or organ dysfunction
cards.detail: Multidisciplinary management including palliative radiation, chemotherapy, or surgery.
